---
title: "1.1 The Importance of Backtesting"
subtitle: "Video lesson slides"
author: "Algorithmic Trading"
notes: |
  Imagine seeing a strategy with an impressive equity curve and deciding to
  trade it, only to learn later that the result depended on a price, timestamp,
  or order assumption you could never reproduce live. That is why backtesting
  comes first. In this lesson, we will treat a backtest not as a marketing
  chart, but as a rehearsal for real trading, and we will see why the details
  of implementation determine whether a historical result is useful or
  misleading.

format:
  revealjs:
    theme: default
    css: ../../../../styles/reveal.css
    slide-number: true
    controls: true
    progress: true
    menu: false
    transition: slide
    transition-speed: fast
    center: false
    auto-stretch: true
    auto-animate: true
    show-notes: false
    scrollable: true
    embed-resources: false
filters:
  - ../../../../scripts/lua/remove-reveal-notes.lua
include-before-body:
  - ../../../../styles/reveal-audience-only.html
execute:
  echo: true
---

## A backtest is useful only when it rehearses the live strategy.

| Mode     | Input           | Output            |
| -------- | --------------- | ----------------- |
| Backtest | historical feed | simulated orders  |
| Live     | market feed     | executable orders |

The feed changes. The strategy rule should not.

::: {.notes}
Start with the core idea of the chapter: a backtest matters only if it
rehearses the strategy we plan to trade. Historical data and live data are two
different feeds, but the trading rule should remain the same. So timing, order
type, venue, and price field all have to be explicit. Otherwise, the backtest
is measuring a strategy that disappears the moment we go live.
:::

## Rehearsal means one strategy rule under two data feeds.

::: {.visual-slide}
::: {.visual-frame}
![](figures/live-strategy-rehearsal.svg){fig-alt="Flow from same strategy logic to same timing rules to same execution assumptions"}
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Now express that same idea as a system diagram. The key question is whether the
same strategy rule survives the move from historical data to live data. If the
logic changes when the feed changes, then the backtest is not validation. It is
just a separate simulation with different assumptions.
:::

## Published performance still hides implementation choices.

| Published phrase    | Execution decision that changes returns           |
| ------------------- | ------------------------------------------------- |
| "buy at the open"   | MOO auction or market order after the open        |
| "use the close"     | primary auction, consolidated last, or settlement |
| "trigger on price"  | bid, ask, last trade, high, or low                |
| "trade S&P futures" | stock close at 4:00 or futures close at 4:15      |

::: {.notes}
That is why we should not accept published performance at face value. A paper
or article usually highlights the idea, not every execution choice behind it.
But those missing choices can change returns materially. "Buy at the open" or
"use the close" sounds precise until we ask which auction, which print, and
which clock. Independent backtesting forces those hidden decisions into the open.
:::

## Independent backtesting turns an idea into an executable rule.

::: {.visual-slide}
::: {.visual-frame}
![](figures/executable-rule-pipeline.svg){fig-alt="Pipeline from published idea to executable rule through instrument, venue, timestamp, order type, backtest code, and execution code"}
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
So independent backtesting does more than verify someone else's number. It
translates an informal trading idea into an executable rule. We move from the
headline concept to concrete choices about instrument, venue, timestamp, and
order type. Once those choices are encoded, the backtest becomes the first
version of an automated trading system rather than a decorative chart.
:::

## Pitfall checks must happen before the equity curve earns trust.

| Strategy setting             | Hidden feasibility check               |
| ---------------------------- | -------------------------------------- |
| Long-short stock book        | Were the short names borrowable?       |
| Intermarket futures pair     | Did both market closes occur together? |
| Published rule after release | Did post-publication data still work?  |

::: {.notes}
Once the rule is explicit, the next step is not admiring the equity curve. The
next step is checking whether the trades were feasible. Could we actually borrow
the short names? Did the two futures markets close at the same time? These
questions come first because most backtesting mistakes make performance look
better, not worse.
:::

## True out-of-sample testing begins after the rule is frozen.

::: {.visual-slide}
::: {.visual-frame}
![](figures/out-of-sample-boundary.svg){fig-alt="Timeline showing research period, publication boundary, and validation period"}
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
This leads directly to the meaning of true out-of-sample testing. A validation
period is not really out of sample if we keep changing the rule after seeing the
result. Once we revise the parameters, that data has become part of research.
Publication creates a cleaner boundary because the rule is effectively frozen,
and later data can test it without the same opportunity for quiet revision.
:::

## Backtesting is an experiment cycle with a hard validation boundary.

::: {.visual-slide}
::: {.visual-frame}
![](figures/backtesting-experiment-cycle.svg){fig-alt="Experiment cycle from hypothesis to implementation to backtest result, revision, frozen rule, and walk-forward test"}
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Seen this way, backtesting is an experiment cycle. We form a hypothesis, code
it, test it, and revise it when the evidence is weak. That iteration is normal
and useful. But it needs a hard stopping point. After the rule is frozen, the
next data should evaluate the strategy, not give us one more chance to tune it.
:::

## A credible backtest can answer three live-trading questions.

| Question                         | Evidence in the backtest               |
| -------------------------------- | -------------------------------------- |
| Can the same rule run live?      | one strategy function, two data feeds  |
| Was the information available?   | signals delayed to decision time       |
| Was the market access realistic? | venue, order, borrow, and clock checks |

::: {.notes}
Close with three review questions that connect everything in the lesson. Can
the same rule run live if we swap in a live feed? Was every signal built from
information available at the decision time? And did the test assume market
access we could actually obtain? If any answer is no, the backtest may still be
interesting research, but it is not yet evidence we can trade.
:::
